3 min read
Just now

--

Took a Break, and It Became the Best Career DecisionAs developers, we often believe that the more time we invest in something, the harder we should fight to save it.
I used to think the same.
Until one project completely changed my perspective.
Everything Started Perfectly
A few months ago, I was assigned a React project at my company. My responsibility was to design and implement an entire module from scratch.
I spent days planning the UI, choosing colors, building reusable components, making the layout responsive, and polishing every little detail. Slowly, the project started looking exactly the way I had imagined.
I was proud of it.
When I finally presented the work, I expected feedback about small improvements.
Instead, my project manager looked at it and simply said:
“This isn’t the direction we’re looking for.”
That sentence hit harder than I expected.
When Hard Work Feels Worthless
At that moment, all I could think about was the countless hours I had spent building the design.
My first reaction was frustration.
I wanted to defend every decision I had made.
I wanted to convince everyone that the design was good.
But deep down, I knew something important.
It didn’t matter how many hours I had invested.
If the project wasn’t solving the right problem, keeping it alive wouldn’t make it any better.
I Closed My Laptop
Instead of rewriting everything while I was angry, I did something unusual.
I closed my laptop.
I went for a walk.
I stopped thinking about React, CSS, components, and deadlines.
Sometimes your brain doesn’t need another hour of coding.
It needs a little space.
Starting Again
The next day, I opened the project with a completely different mindset.
This time I didn’t try to save the old design.
I deleted large parts of the code.
Yes, it was painful.
Watching days of work disappear isn’t easy for any developer.
But something interesting happened.
Without trying to protect my previous work, I started making better decisions.
The code became cleaner.
The layout became simpler.
The user experience improved.
Most importantly, I was designing for the product — not for my ego.
The Unexpected Result
A few days later, I presented the new version.
This time the response was completely different.
Everyone loved it.
The project manager appreciated the new direction.
Even better, that project opened another opportunity inside the company.
I was trusted with a new project where I handled both the development and the UI design.
Looking back, if I had kept trying to “fix” my first version, I probably would have missed that opportunity.
The Biggest Lesson
As developers, we often become emotionally attached to our code.
We think:

• “I’ve already spent three days on this.”

• “I’ve already written thousands of lines of code.”

• “I can’t delete it now.”
But computers don’t care how long you worked.
Users don’t care how difficult your code was.
Clients don’t care how attached you are to your implementation.
They only care about the final result.
Sometimes Progress Looks Like Going Back
Taking a short break isn’t wasting time.
Deleting code isn’t always failure.
Starting over doesn’t mean you lost.
Sometimes those are exactly the things that move you forward.
The same lesson applies outside programming.
Whether you’re learning a new skill, building a business, preparing for exams, or simply trying to improve your life, there are moments when the best decision isn’t to push harder.
It’s to pause.
Breathe.
Think clearly.
Then begin again with a fresh mind.
Final Thoughts
Today, I don’t measure progress by how many hours I spend coding.
I measure it by how much value I create.
Sometimes consistency means working every day.
Sometimes consistency means knowing when to stop, recharge, and return stronger.
Because the best version of your work rarely comes from a tired mind.
It comes from a clear one.
Have you ever deleted hours — or even days — of work and ended up building something much better? I’d love to hear your story in the comments.
